content:
# Ruth Chapter 1 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 880–881 | **Source Image:** RUT_001_page_001.jpg, RUT_001_page_002.jpg

---

**1** Back in the days when judges ruled, a famine struck the land. A man from Bethlehem in Judah took his wife and two sons and went to live as a foreigner in the country of Moab.

**2** The man's name was Elimelech, his wife's name was Naomi, and his two sons were called Mahlon and Chilion. They were Ephrathites from Bethlehem in Judah. They arrived in Moab and settled there.

**3** Then Elimelech, Naomi's husband, died, and she was left with her two sons.

**4** The sons married Moabite women — one named Orpah, the other Ruth. They lived there for about ten years.

**5** Then both Mahlon and Chilion also died, and Naomi was left without her two children and without her husband.

**6** While still in Moab, she heard that the LORD had turned his attention back to his people and given them food. So she set out with her daughters-in-law to return from Moab.

**7** She left the place where she had been living, and the two daughters-in-law went with her. They started down the road that would take them back to the land of Judah.

**8** But Naomi said to her two daughters-in-law, "Go back, both of you — return to your mothers' homes. May the LORD show you the same loyal love you have shown to the dead and to me.

**9** May the LORD grant each of you the security of a new husband's home." She kissed them, and they broke down weeping.

**10** They said to her, "No! We will go back with you, to your people."

**11** But Naomi said, "Turn back, my daughters. Why would you come with me? Do I still have sons in my womb who could become your husbands?

**12** Turn back, my daughters — go. I am too old to have another husband. Even if I thought there was still hope for me, even if I lay with a husband tonight and bore sons —

**13** would you really wait for them to grow up? Would you shut yourselves away and refuse to marry? No, my daughters. My lot is far more bitter than yours, because the LORD's own hand has turned against me."

**14** Again they wept loudly. Orpah kissed her mother-in-law goodbye, but Ruth held on to her and would not let go.

**15** Naomi said, "Look — your sister-in-law has gone back to her people and to her gods. Go back with her."

**16** But Ruth said, "Don't ask me to leave you or to turn back from following you. Wherever you go, I will go. Wherever you stay, I will stay. Your people will be my people, and your God will be my God.

**17** Where you die, I will die, and there I will be buried. May the LORD deal with me, and worse, if anything but death separates me from you."

**18** When Naomi saw that Ruth was absolutely determined to go with her, she stopped arguing.

**19** So the two of them walked on until they reached Bethlehem. When they arrived, the whole town was buzzing with excitement. The women exclaimed, "Can this really be Naomi?"

**20** She told them, "Don't call me Naomi — call me Mara, because the Almighty has made my life utterly bitter.

**21** I left here full, and the LORD has brought me back empty. Why call me Naomi? The LORD has testified against me; the Almighty has brought disaster upon me."

**22** So Naomi came home, and with her came Ruth the Moabite, her daughter-in-law, returning from the country of Moab. They arrived in Bethlehem just as the barley harvest was beginning.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 — "Back in the days when judges ruled":** The Hebrew וַיְהִי בִּימֵי שְׁפֹט הַשֹּׁפְטִים sets the story in a turbulent, lawless era. The dynamic rendering "Back in the days" captures the narrative's fairy-tale-like opening while preserving historical grounding.

**Verse 8 — "loyal love" for חֶסֶד (hesed):** This untranslatable Hebrew word carries covenant faithfulness, loyal love, and steadfast kindness all at once. "Loyal love" conveys both the relational and volitional dimensions — this is love that chooses to remain faithful.

**Verse 13 — "My lot is far more bitter than yours":** The Hebrew כִּי־מַר־לִי מְאֹד מִכֶּם expresses Naomi's grief as exceeding even theirs. She sees herself as the one whom God has singled out for suffering, not them.

**Verse 14 — "held on to her and would not let go":** The Hebrew דָּבְקָה (davqah) means to cling, to bond inseparably. It is the same word used for marriage in Genesis 2:24. Ruth's attachment to Naomi is rendered with covenantal intensity.

**Verses 16–17 — Ruth's declaration:** This is one of Scripture's most celebrated speeches. The Hebrew parallelism builds with cumulative force. The dynamic rendering preserves the rhythm of commitment — place, people, God, death, burial — as an ascending oath of total devotion. Ruth, a Moabite foreigner, chooses Israel's God and Israel's people with an irrevocable vow.

**Verse 20 — "Naomi" and "Mara":** Naomi (נָעֳמִי) means "my pleasantness" or "my delight." Mara (מָרָא) means "bitter." The name change captures her theological interpretation of her own suffering — God has turned sweetness into bitterness.

— PNEUMA
